docs.title: Fundación Columbus Rare Diseases
docs.text:
Welcome! This site consolidates disease research sources in one place.

Use the navigation menu (or the search bar above) to browse by disease.

All original source documents are also backed up in SharePoint for full traceability.

docs.title: Disease Overview
docs.text: 10p15.3 microdeletion syndrome is a rare genetic condition characterized by the loss of genetic material on the short arm of chromosome 10. The syndrome is typically categorized into two groups: patients with a smaller deletion restricted to the 10p15.3 region and those with a larger deletion involving the 10p15.3–15.1 region. While clinical differences between these groups are not significantly distinct, the smaller deletion region contains critical genes such as ZMYND11 and DIP2C. Loss-of-function mutations in ZMYND11 specifically cause ZMYND11-related syndromic intellectual disability (ZRSID), a disorder where the protein's role as a "chromatin reader" is disrupted, leading to aberrant gene expression during brain development.

docs.title: Epidemiology/Prevalence
docs.text: This is an extremely rare condition; prior to recent research published in 2025, only 26 cases had been reported in medical literature.

docs.title: Clinical Features
docs.text: The clinical presentation of 10p15.3 microdeletion syndrome is diverse and includes: - Developmental and Intellectual Impact: Global developmental delay (DD), intellectual disability (ID), and language and motor delays. - Behavioral Symptoms: Hyperactivity, repetitive behaviors, and general behavioral disruptions. - Physical Characteristics: Abnormal facial features, short stature, and deformities of the hands and feet. - Neurological Issues: Hypotonia (low muscle tone) and seizures. - Congenital Heart Defects (CHDs): Observed in approximately 19.2% of patients, often involving genes like DIP2C.

docs.title: Diagnosis
docs.text: Diagnosis is typically achieved through detailed genetic analysis, such as chromosomal microarray or sequencing, to identify a de novo deletion at the 10p15.3 locus. Clinical suspicion may initially lean toward other conditions; for instance, some patients have been originally suspected of having Williams syndrome before genetic testing confirmed the 10p15.3 microdeletion. Diagnosis may also identify specific haploinsufficiency or missense variants of the ZMYND11 gene.

docs.title: Natural History
docs.text: Not addressed in available sources. While sources describe symptoms present in early childhood (such as a case report of a 1-year-old male), they do not provide a comprehensive longitudinal account of the disease progression across a full human lifespan.

docs.title: Therapeutic Landscape
docs.text: There are currently no disease-specific approved therapies for 10p15.3 microdeletion syndrome or ZRSID 2. Management typically focuses on addressing individual symptoms like seizures or heart defects. However, recent research has identified the KMT2A (MLL1) protein as a potential therapeutic target, as ZMYND11 normally functions to inhibit KMT2A activity. The drug revumenib, which is approved for certain types of leukemia, has shown potential in preclinical models to attenuate gene expression changes caused by ZMYND11 loss.

docs.title: Research
docs.text:
Preclinical Research: - Zebrafish Models: Used to confirm that deficiency of the DIP2C gene leads to heart defects similar to those seen in human patients. - Mouse Models: Researchers utilized Zmynd11 conditional deletion mice to observe reduced dendritic branching and spine density in neurons, as well as hyperactivity and impaired motor coordination. - Mechanistic Studies: Studies have discovered that ZMYND11 interacts with the chromatin regulator KMT2A; when ZMYND11 is mutated, this interaction is lost, leading to the upregulation of non-neuronal gene programs.

Clinical Research: Not addressed in available sources. The provided sources do not list specific active clinical trials enrolling children for this syndrome.

docs.title: Bibliography
docs.text: Xia et al. (2025): "The deficiency of DIP2C leads to congenital heart defects in patients with 10p15.3 microdeletion syndrome." This study explores the genetic mechanisms of heart defects in the syndrome, specifically identifying DIP2C as a candidate gene through zebrafish experiments Greben et al. (Recent/Undated): "ZMYND11 Restrains KMT2A to Enable a Neuronal Developmental Program." This research identifies the molecular interaction between ZMYND11 and KMT2A, suggesting that KMT2A inhibitors could be a future treatment for ZRSID Note: None of the sources used in this profile are over 3 years old based on the provided publication dates (2025) and contemporary references to drugs approved in 2024.

docs.title: Disease Overview
docs.text: Alveolar rhabdomyosarcoma (ARMS) is a rare and aggressive pediatric soft tissue malignancy that arises from skeletal muscle tissue. It is the second most common subtype of rhabdomyosarcoma (RMS), a cancer that accounts for approximately 3% of all childhood malignant tumors. ARMS is characterized by a specific histological pattern and is frequently associated with chromosomal translocations that create oncogenic fusion proteins. Based on risk stratification, ARMS is typically categorized as an intermediate or high-risk disease, with significantly poorer outcomes for high-risk and metastatic cases.

docs.title: Epidemiology/Prevalence
docs.text: The incidence of RMS in individuals under the age of 20 is approximately 4.5 patients per million in the United States, which translates to about 350 new cases annually. ARMS specifically has a prevalence of approximately 1.0 case per million population. While RMS follows a bimodal age distribution overall, ARMS tends to occur more frequently in late childhood and adolescence. There is a slight male predominance in childhood RMS generally, though specific gender predominance for the ARMS subtype is not always clearly defined. Geographically, incidence rates are stable globally, except in East Asia (Japan, India, and China), where the incidence is lower at approximately 2 cases per million.

docs.title: Clinical Features
docs.text: ARMS primary tumors are most commonly found on the trunk and extremities, though they can also occur in the inferior orbit of the eye. Clinically, ARMS is often more aggressive than the embryonal subtype (ERMS). Approximately 15–20% of children diagnosed with RMS have diffused metastatic disease at the time of initial presentation, which is a significant factor in prognostic stratification. Patients with high-risk ARMS lesions face a 5-year survival rate of less than 30%.

docs.title: How It's Diagnosed
docs.text:
Diagnosis of ARMS involves a combination of histological, immunohistochemical, and molecular techniques:

  • Histology: ARMS is identified by densely packed, small, round cells that line septations, creating a pattern that resembles fetal alveoli
  • Immunohistochemistry: The presence of skeletal muscle proteins—such as alpha-actin, MyoD1, myogenin, and desmin—is used to differentiate ARMS from other small round blue cell tumors like neuroblastoma or Ewing sarcoma
  • Molecular Biology: The majority (80%) of ARMS cases are "fusion-positive," involving a balanced translocation between chromosomes 2 and 13 (PAX3-FOXO1, found in 60% of cases) or chromosomes 1 and 13 (PAX7-FOXO1, found in 20% of cases). Identifying these fusions is critical, as fusion-negative ARMS often behaves clinically more like the more favorable ERMS subtype
  • Imaging: Diagnostic procedures used in clinical settings and trials include Computed Tomography (CT), Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI), Positron Emission Tomography (PET), and bone scans

docs.title: Natural History
docs.text: The natural history of ARMS is marked by aggressive local growth and a high potential for distant metastasis. The disease progression is heavily influenced by fusion status; PAX3-FOXO1 positive cases often occur at an older age and carry a worse prognosis compared to PAX7-FOXO1 positive cases. Without effective multi-agent chemotherapy, recurrence rates are as high as 50–60%. For patients who relapse, the 5-year survival rate drops significantly to approximately 17%.

docs.title: Therapeutic Landscape
docs.text:
Standard treatment for ARMS requires a multimodal approach involving systemic chemotherapy, surgical resection, and radiation therapy.

  • Chemotherapy: In North America, the standard "VAC" regimen consists of vincristine, dactinomycin, and cyclophosphamide. European centers often use a similar "IVA" regime (ifosfamide, vincristine, and actinomycin D). Maintenance therapy with vinorelbine and low-dose cyclophosphamide has shown promise in improving survival for high-risk patients
  • Surgery: Complete surgical resection with margins of 0.5–1 cm is preferred for localized disease, though the extent of surgery must be balanced against functional and cosmetic outcomes in children
  • Radiation: Radiotherapy (including intensity-modulated radiation therapy and proton beam therapy) or brachytherapy is used for local control, particularly in cases with microscopic or gross residual disease

docs.title: Research (Preclinical and Clinical)
docs.text:
Preclinical Research:

  • Animal Models: Researchers utilize cell-line-derived xenografts (CDX), patient-derived xenografts (PDX), and genetically engineered mouse models (GEMM) to study drug mechanisms and tumor maintenance. Zebrafish models are also used for high-throughput drug screening and tracking metastasis
  • Molecular Targets: Studies are exploring the role of the Unfolded Protein Response (UPR), specifically the IRE1 and PERK pathways, which are constitutively active in ARMS and support cell survival. Targeting autophagy and apoptosis pathways (such as Bcl-2 family proteins or PLK1) is another area of active investigation to overcome chemoresistance

Clinical Research (Pediatric Focus):

  • NCT06669013: A recruiting trial evaluating chemo-immunotherapy (dinutuximab beta) in patients under 18 with advanced/metastatic GD2-positive RMS
  • NCT04994132: A phase III trial comparing the addition of vinorelbine to the VAC backbone and the use of maintenance therapy for high-risk RMS
  • NCT05304585: A phase III trial for newly diagnosed patients, examining centralized molecular risk stratification and therapy intensification for mutated cases
  • NCT02748135: A completed study of TB-403 (anti-PlGF antibody) in pediatric subjects with relapsed or refractory ARMS
  • EudraCT 2022-002793-91: A prospective phase I/II trial of an individualized peptide vaccine for pediatric and AYA patients with metastasized fusion-driven sarcomas, including ARMS

docs.title: Bibliography
docs.text:
Zarrabi et al. (2023). Rhabdomyosarcoma: Current Therapy, Challenges, and Future Approaches to Treatment Strategies. Published in Cancers. This comprehensive review provides a clinical understanding of RMS subtypes, diagnostic methods, current treatments, and emerging research into cell death pathways and bioengineering models.

ctg-studies (4).csv (Data retrieved 2026). A compilation of clinical trial records from ClinicalTrials.gov. It includes summaries, statuses, and intervention details for ongoing and completed trials targeting rhabdomyosarcoma.

trials-summary (3).txt (Data retrieved 2026). A summary of European clinical trials (EudraCT). This source details phase I-III trials in Europe focusing on translocation-related sarcomas and novel therapies like peptide vaccines and crizotinib.

Note on Source Age: The primary review source (Zarrabi et al.) was published in late 2023 and is approximately 2.7 years old as of today's date. Some clinical trial records referenced from the CSV and Markdown files include studies initiated as early as 2007 (e.g., NCT00354835) or 2011, which are over 3 years old.

docs.title: Disease Overview
docs.text: CDKL5 Deficiency Disorder (CDD) is a developmental and epileptic encephalopathy (DEE). It is caused by pathogenic variants in the CDKL5 gene, located on the X chromosome, which encodes a serine-threonine kinase essential for normal brain development and function. The disorder is characterized by severe early-onset intractable epilepsy and significant disturbances in motor, cognitive, visual, and autonomic functions. Historically, it was considered a variant of Rett syndrome, but it is now recognized as a distinct clinical entity

docs.title: Epidemiology/Prevalence
docs.text: While the exact prevalence in the general population is unknown, the estimated incidence is approximately 2.36 in 100,000 live births. The disorder affects females more frequently than males, with a female-to-male ratio of approximately 4:1. Despite this, the clinical severity can be equivalent between sexes, though phenotypic variation occurs due to factors like X-chromosome inactivation patterns in females and mosaicism in both sexes

docs.title: Clinical Features
docs.text:
• Epilepsy: Typically begins within the first two months of life (up to 12 months) and is usually severe and refractory to standard medications Seizure types vary and include infantile spasms, tonic, and focal seizures
  • Developmental Delay: All affected individuals have impaired gross motor, fine motor, and speech-language development. Approximately 20% achieve independent walking and 20% develop spoken language.
  • Cerebral Visual Impairment (CVI): Affects 80% of individuals, manifesting as abnormal eye movements (nystagmus), poor tracking, and abnormal responsiveness to light.
  • Movement Disorders: Includes hand and leg stereotypies (e.g., mouthing, flapping), chorea, and dystonia.
  • Autonomic & Gastrointestinal: Common issues include sleep disturbances (90%), constipation, and gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD).
  • Musculoskeletal: Generalized hypotonia is common, often leading to scoliosis or joint abnormalities.

docs.title: Diagnosis
docs.text: The diagnosis is established through molecular genetic testing that identifies a heterozygous (in females) or hemizygous (in males) CDKL5 pathogenic variant. - Suggestive Findings: Clinicians should suspect CDD in infants with motor/cognitive delays and epilepsy onset within the first year - Testing Approaches: Multigene panels for epilepsy are the most common diagnostic tool, with sequence analysis identifying about 84% of variants and deletion/duplication analysis identifying the remaining 16% - Advanced Testing: If initial panels are negative, exome or genome sequencing may be used to find rare intronic or noncoding variants

docs.title: Natural History
docs.text: Symptoms typically emerge in early infancy, with seizures often being the first sign. Some children (up to 40%) experience a "honeymoon period" around ages one to two where seizure frequency temporarily decreases before returning. Brain MRIs are often normal in the first year but frequently show progressive cortical and cerebellar atrophy as the child ages. Developmental impairments and medical comorbidities, such as GI and sleep issues, are typically lifelong

docs.title: Therapeutic Landscape
docs.text: There is currently no cure for CDD. Management is multidisciplinary and focused on supportive care. - Targeted Therapy: Ganaxolone (Ztalmy®) is the first and only FDA-approved targeted therapy specifically for seizures associated with CDD in patients aged two years and older. - Standard Management: Refractory seizures are managed with various anti-seizure medications (ASMs), though they are often ineffective. - Supportive Care: Therapies include PT/OT, speech-language therapy, and nutritional support; approximately 30% of individuals require a gastrostomy tube for feeding. - Other Interventions: The ketogenic diet and vagus nerve stimulation have been used with variable success for seizure management.

docs.title: Research (Preclinical and Clinical)
docs.text:
Preclinical: Research focuses on understanding the kinase function of CDKL5 and its role in excitatory synapse stability. Protein and gene replacement therapies are currently under investigation

Clinical (Childhood-Focused): - Ganaxolone: A completed Phase 3 trial (NCT03572933) demonstrated efficacy in children and young adults. A new interventional trial is recruiting/planned for children aged 6 months to 2 years (NCT05249556). - Fenfluramine: Clinical studies (NCT03861871, NCT05064878) have investigated its effect on convulsive seizures in CDD. - Soticlestat: The Phase 2 ARCADE study (NCT03694275) included pediatric participants with CDD to evaluate seizure reduction. - Observational Research: Several ongoing studies and registries (NCT05558371, NCT04486768, NCT05373719) are tracking the natural history and long-term outcomes of the disorder to inform future treatments. - Novel Interventions: Studies are evaluating hyperthermic baths (NCT06447675, NCT07602205) and gut-targeted dietary supplements (NCT06448663) for seizure control.

docs.title: Bibliography
docs.text: CDKL5 Deficiency Disorder (GeneReviews), 2024 (Updated 2025). A comprehensive clinical reference detailing the genetic causes, diagnostic criteria, management protocols, and phenotypic spectrum of CDD. CTG-Studies (2).csv, 2026. A dataset of clinical trials and observational studies from ClinicalTrials.gov, providing current data on investigational drugs, registries, and therapeutic research for CDD. Note: None of the primary sources used for this profile are over 3 years old (as of July 2026).

docs.title: Disease Overview
docs.text: ECHS1 (short-chain enoyl-CoA hydratase) deficiency is a rare mitochondrial disorder caused by mutations in the ECHS1 gene located on chromosome 10q26.3. This gene encodes a mitochondrial matrix enzyme essential for fatty acid β-oxidation and branched-chain amino acid (BCAA) metabolism, specifically the degradation of valine 1-3. Mutations typically lead to reduced or absent enzyme activity, causing mitochondrial dysfunction, energy failure in high-demand tissues, and the accumulation of toxic metabolic intermediates.

docs.title: Epidemiology/Prevalence
docs.text: The exact prevalence is not addressed in available sources, though it is characterized broadly as a rare metabolic and neurodegenerative disorder.

docs.title: Clinical Features
docs.text:
The clinical presentation is highly heterogeneous but typically begins in the neonatal or early infancy period. Key manifestations include:

  • Neurological: Hypotonia, delayed motor development, seizures, dystonia, and neurodevelopmental regression.
  • Leigh Syndrome: Many patients present with Leigh syndrome, characterized by early-onset encephalopathy and bilateral basal ganglia lesions.
  • Metabolic: Significant abnormalities such as lactic acidosis and elevated pyruvate levels in blood and cerebrospinal fluid.
  • Systemic: Potential for cardiomyopathy, optic atrophy, and movement disorders. Some associations have even been suggested with increased susceptibility to certain cancers due to oncometabolite accumulation.

docs.title: Diagnosis
docs.text:
• Neuroimaging (MRI): A crucial tool that typically reveals bilateral T2 hyperintensity in the basal ganglia. Other findings can include white matter damage and thinning of the corpus callosum.
  • Biochemical Screening: Detection of elevated lactate and pyruvate. Urinary metabolite analysis is a potential screening tool to detect abnormal metabolites such as S-(2-carboxypropyl)cysteine and crotonyl glycine.
  • Genetic Testing: Diagnosis is confirmed through identifying pathogenic mutations (missense, nonsense, splice-site, or frameshift) in the ECHS1 gene via genome sequencing.

docs.title: Natural History
docs.text: Symptoms generally appear early in life and tend to worsen as the disease progresses, leading to further motor and cognitive regression. Severe mitochondrial energy failure can result in early lethality, a fact supported by animal models where complete gene knockout often leads to embryonic or early postnatal death.

docs.title: Therapeutic Landscape
docs.text:
Currently, treatments primarily focus on symptomatic support and metabolic stabilization.

  • Dietary Interventions: Restricting BCAA intake or implementing a low-protein diet can help reduce the accumulation of toxic metabolites.
  • Pharmacological Support: Antioxidants such as Coenzyme Q10 and Vitamin E may be used to mitigate oxidative stress and improve mitochondrial function.
  • Symptom Management: Targeted management for seizures and other neurological symptoms.

docs.title: Research
docs.text:
Preclinical: Research is active across several models

  • Animal Models: Heterozygous and conditional knockout mice are used to study tissue-specific impacts (e.g., in the heart and nervous system), and Drosophila models help replicate metabolic disruptions.
  • Cellular Models: Induced pluripotent stem cells (iPSCs) from patients are used to recapitulate cellular phenotypes and for drug screening.

Emerging Strategies: Investigations into AAV-based gene therapy to restore enzyme activity, Enzyme Replacement Therapy (ERT) using mitochondrial targeting signals, and Exosome therapy to deliver mRNA or proteins across the blood-brain barrier are underway.

Clinical: Clinical research is currently focused on clarifying pathophysiological roles and identifying novel mutation types to improve early diagnosis. Specific clinical trials enrolling children were not addressed in the available sources.

docs.title: Bibliography
docs.text:
Fu et al. (2025): "ECHS1: pathogenic mechanisms, experimental models, and emerging therapeutic strategies," published in Orphanet Journal of Rare Diseases. This source provides a comprehensive review of the ECHS1 gene's function in energy metabolism, the clinical features of deficiency (including Leigh syndrome), diagnostic markers, and a detailed evaluation of current preclinical research and potential future therapies.

Note: None of the primary source material used for this profile is over 3 years old (based on the publication date of 2025).

docs.title: Disease Overview
docs.text: Severe combined immunodeficiency (SCID) is a rare and life-threatening genetic disorder that severely impairs the immune system's ability to defend the body against infections. Often referred to as "bubble boy" disease, it results from genetic mutations—of which over 20 are known—that disrupt the development and function of immune cells, primarily T cells and B cells. Because the immune system is virtually non-existent, infants with SCID are highly susceptible to recurrent, severe infections from viruses, bacteria, and fungi. It is considered a pediatric emergency because it is fatal without prompt treatment.

docs.title: Epidemiology/Prevalence
docs.text:
• Incidence: Historically estimated at 1:75,000 to 1:100,000 individuals. However, following the implementation of newborn screening in the USA, the incidence has been found to be higher, approximately 1:58,000 births
  • Common Forms: The most common form is X-linked SCID (SCID-X1), which accounts for approximately 40–50% of all cases. Other common types include those caused by mutations in the ADA, IL7RA, and RAG1 genes
  • Regional Data: In Latin America, the lack of universal neonatal screening in many countries often leads to underdiagnosis and delayed referral.

docs.title: Clinical Features
docs.text:
The clinical presentation typically occurs in the first few months of life. Key features include:

  • Recurrent/Severe Infections: Persistent respiratory tract infections, pneumonia (often caused by Pneumocystis jirovecii or CMV), and disseminated infections from live vaccines like BCG
  • Gastrointestinal Issues: Chronic diarrhea and persistent oral or intestinal candidiasis (thrush)
  • Failure to Thrive: Infants usually have a normal birth weight but deviate from the expected growth curve once infections begin
  • Skin Manifestations: Persistent skin rashes; specific forms like Omenn syndrome may present with generalized erythematous rash, enlarged lymph nodes, and hepatosplenomegaly

docs.title: Diagnosis
docs.text:
• Newborn Screening (NBS): The primary method for early identification, utilizing the quantification of T-cell receptor excision circles (TRECs) from dried blood spots to detect T-cell lymphopenia
  • Laboratory Tests:
  • Complete Blood Count: A lymphocyte count below 2.8 x 10⁹/L in childhood strongly suggests SCID
  • Flow Cytometry: Essential for identifying the specific immunological phenotype (e.g., T-B+NK- or T-B-NK+) based on the presence or absence of lymphocyte subtypes
  • Genetic Testing: Next-generation sequencing is used to identify the specific genetic mutation, which is critical for tailoring treatment
  • Diagnostic Criteria: Patients are categorized into Typical SCID (very low T cells, <0.05 x 10⁹/L), Leaky/Atypical SCID (reduced but present T cells with impaired function), or Omenn Syndrome

docs.title: Natural History
docs.text: Without definitive treatment, classic SCID is universally fatal, with most patients succumbing to severe opportunistic infections before their first birthday. Early diagnosis through screening significantly improves the prognosis by allowing for intervention before the onset of life-threatening complications.

docs.title: Therapeutic Landscape
docs.text:
• Hematopoietic Stem Cell Transplantation (HSCT): The primary curative treatment. Success rates are highest (over 90%) when performed within the first 3.5 months of life and when an HLA-matched sibling donor is available
  • Enzyme Replacement Therapy (ERT): For patients with ADA-SCID, weekly injections of PEG-ADA (Adagen) can restore some immunity but are not a permanent cure and require lifelong administration
  • Gene Therapy: Explored as a promising alternative for patients lacking a matched donor. It involves modifying the patient's own stem cells to express a functional copy of the defective gene

docs.title: Research
docs.text:
Preclinical Research: Investigation is ongoing into safer viral vectors (transitioning from gammaretroviral to lentiviral vectors) to reduce the risk of insertional mutagenesis, which previously caused leukemia in some trials. Other areas include gene editing (e.g., CRISPR/Cas9), exon skipping using antisense oligonucleotides, and readthrough therapy for nonsense mutations.

Clinical Research (Focus on Children):

  • X-Linked SCID: Trials are evaluating lentiviral gene transfer in newly diagnosed infants (NCT01512888) and base-edited stem cell transplants in those aged 3 and older (NCT06851767)
  • ADA-SCID: Several studies (NCT03765632, NCT02999984) have assessed the efficacy of a cryopreserved formulation of gene-modified stem cells (OTL-101) in children up to 18 years old
  • Artemis-Deficient SCID: A trial is currently recruiting newly diagnosed infants to test autologous gene therapy using a lentiviral vector (NCT03538899)
  • RAG1-Deficient SCID: Clinical trials are enrolling children up to 24 months of age for autologous stem cell gene therapy (NCT04797260, EudraCT 2019-002343-14)

docs.title: Bibliography
docs.text:
Aranda, C. S., et al. (2024). Severe combined immunodeficiency diagnosis and genetic defects. This review article provides a comprehensive overview of SCID, focusing on its genetic causes, clinical manifestations, and the critical role of newborn screening and early diagnosis.

scid-ctg-studies.csv (Accessed 2026). A dataset containing detailed information on various interventional and observational clinical trials for SCID, primarily sourced from ClinicalTrials.gov.

scid-trials-summary.txt (Accessed 2026). A summary of clinical trial records from the European Union Clinical Trials Register, detailing study titles, medical conditions, and participating age groups for various SCID gene therapy protocols.

Note: The clinical trial data in scid-trials-summary.txt and portions of scid-ctg-studies.csv include records and study start dates (e.g., EudraCT entries from 2010–2021) that are over 3 years old relative to the current date of July 2026.

docs.title: Disease Overview
docs.text: Surfactant protein B (SP-B) deficiency is a rare inherited disorder that causes interstitial lung disease. It is characterized by the body's inability to produce functional surfactant—a mixture of fats and proteins that reduces surface tension in the lungs to prevent alveolar collapse during exhalation. The condition is caused by variants in the SFTPB gene and is inherited in an autosomal recessive pattern, meaning an affected child must inherit a disease-causing gene variant from each parent. Synonyms for the condition include SP-B deficiency, pulmonary surfactant metabolism dysfunction-1 (SMDP1), and congenital pulmonary alveolar proteinosis.

docs.title: Epidemiology/Prevalence
docs.text: In the United States, the estimated incidence of surfactant B protein deficiency is 1 in 1 million live births.

docs.title: Clinical Features
docs.text:
Infants with SP-B deficiency may appear healthy immediately after birth, but they typically develop severe respiratory distress within hours. Characteristic features include:

  • Tachypnea (increased breathing rate)
  • Cyanosis (gray or blue skin pallor)
  • Grunting and nasal flaring
  • Increased oxygen demand and breathing effort

While most cases are severe, there are reports of milder lung disease in children with variants that cause only a partial deficiency.

docs.title: Diagnosis
docs.text:
A diagnosis is suspected in infants whose respiratory distress does not improve after five days of life.

  • Definitive Diagnosis: Confirmed through genetic testing to identify variants in the SFTPB gene
  • Supportive Testing: High-resolution CT scans of the chest, bronchoscopy with bronchoalveolar lavage (lung washing), infant pulmonary function testing, and lung biopsies are used to aid the diagnosis
  • Prenatal Testing: Possible during future pregnancies via amniotic fluid or chorionic villus sampling

docs.title: Natural History
docs.text: The condition is extremely serious and progresses rapidly. Without a lung transplant, the disease usually leads to fatal respiratory failure within three to six months of life. Interventions such as surfactant replacement therapy typically only provide temporary relief, as the symptoms continue to worsen over time.

docs.title: Therapeutic Landscape
docs.text:
Currently, no specific medical treatments exist for SP-B deficiency.

  • Definitive Treatment: Lung transplantation is the only definitive therapy, though it carries high risks for severe health problems in infants
  • Supportive Care: Management focuses on symptom monitoring and temporary measures. Surfactant replacement therapy may offer short-term stabilization
  • Off-label/Experimental Stabilizers: In children with milder forms, corticosteroids and hydroxychloroquine have been used to attempt to stabilize the disease until a transplant can be performed

docs.title: Research
docs.text:
Preclinical Research: Not addressed in available sources.

Clinical Research (Pediatric Focus):

  • NCT04056741: A pilot study explored the feasibility and safety of administering surfactant (Infasurf®) through a new supraglottic device in neonates weighing 500 to 5000 grams, including those with SP-B deficiency. This study is currently listed as suspended
  • NCT00828243: A completed observational study investigated the genetic regulation of surfactant deficiency by linking specific genetic code changes in SP-B, SP-C, and ABCA3 genes to disrupted surfactant metabolism in newborn infants

docs.title: Bibliography
docs.text:
Surfactant Protein B Deficiency - Symptoms, Causes, Treatment | NORD (2025): A comprehensive medical report detailing the genetic causes, clinical presentation, diagnostic procedures, and current treatment options for SP-B deficiency.

ctg-studies (5).csv (Date not provided): A collection of clinical trial data from ClinicalTrials.gov regarding interventions and observational studies for respiratory distress syndrome and surfactant deficiencies.

Note: The sources used include clinical trial data (Source 2) with registration dates as early as 2009, which is over 3 years old. The primary NORD report (Source 1) was updated in 2025.
